sentences.base_language: Good morning. I'd like to see the doctor please.
sentences.target_language: صباح الخير. أريد أن أرى الطبيب من فضلك.
sentences.romanized: sabah al-khayr. urid an ara at-tabib min fadlak.
sentences.literal: Morning the-good. I-want that I-see the-doctor from your-favour.
sentences.grammarNote: صَبَاح الخَير (ṣabāḥ al-khayr, good morning) — an iḍāfa greeting: صَبَاح (morning) + الخَير (goodness/the good). The response is صَبَاح النُّور (ṣabāḥ an-nūr, morning of light). أُرِيد أَن أَرى (urīd an arā, I want to see) — Form IV of ر-و-ي/أ-ر-ي: أَرَى (I see) is a defective verb; the ى appears in the subjunctive. الطَّبِيب (aṭ-ṭabīb, the doctor) — فَعِيل pattern from root ط-ب-ب (to practise medicine).

sentences.base_language: Please have a seat. The doctor will see you shortly.
sentences.target_language: تفضل بالجلوس. الطبيب سيراك بعد قليل.
sentences.romanized: tafaddal bil-julus. at-tabib sa-yarak ba'd qalil.
sentences.literal: Be-welcome with-the-sitting. The-doctor will-see-you after little.
sentences.grammarNote: تَفَضَّل بِالجُلُوس (tafaḍḍal bil-julūs, please have a seat) — تَفَضَّل (Form V imperative of ف-ض-ل, to be gracious) + بِالجُلُوس (with the sitting — verbal noun). سَيَرَاك (sa-yarāk, he will see you) — سَ (near future) + يَرَاك: Form I of ر-أ-ي (r-ʾ-y, to see) with the suffix كَ (you). بَعدَ قَلِيل (baʿda qalīl, shortly/after a little) — a common temporal expression: 'after a little while.'

sentences.base_language: Hello. What seems to be the problem?
sentences.grammarNote: ما الشَّكوى؟ (mā ash-shakwā, what is the complaint?) — a nominal question with no verb: مَا (what) + definite noun = 'what is X?' الشَّكوى (ash-shakwā, the complaint/ailment) — from root ش-ك-و (sh-k-w, to complain): شَكَا (he complained), شَكوى (a complaint/grievance). This is a very common clinical opening phrase in Arabic-speaking medical settings.

sentences.base_language: I have a bad headache and a sore throat.
sentences.target_language: عندي صداع شديد وألم في الحلق.
sentences.literal: At-me headache severe and-pain in the-throat.
sentences.grammarNote: عِندي + [symptom] is the natural way to describe symptoms: عِندي صُداع (I have a headache), عِندي حُمَّى (I have a fever), عِندي سُعال (I have a cough). The preposition عِند means 'at/with' and functions as 'to have' for states and conditions.

sentences.base_language: How long have you had these symptoms?
sentences.target_language: منذ متى وأنت تعاني من هذه الأعراض؟
sentences.romanized: mundhu mata wa-anta tu'ani min hadhihi al-a'rad?
sentences.literal: Since when and-you suffering from these the-symptoms?
sentences.grammarNote: مُنذُ مَتى (mundhu matā, since when/how long) — مُنذُ (since/for, used with time) + مَتى (when). وَأَنتَ تُعَانِي (wa-anta tuʿānī, and you are suffering) — a ḥāliyya clause (circumstantial clause): 'while you are suffering.' تُعَانِي from Form III of ع-ن-ي (to suffer/mean): عَانَى = to suffer/endure. الأَعرَاض (al-aʿrāḍ, the symptoms) — broken plural of عَرَض (symptom/sign), pattern أَفعَال.

sentences.base_language: Since yesterday evening. I also feel very tired.
sentences.target_language: منذ أمس المساء. وأيضاً أشعر بتعب شديد.
sentences.romanized: mundhu ams al-masa'. wa-aydan ash'ur bi-ta'ab shadid.
sentences.literal: Since yesterday the-evening. And-also I-feel with-tiredness severe.
sentences.grammarNote: أَمسِ المَسَاء (amsi al-masāʾ, yesterday evening) — أَمس (yesterday) is an indeclinable noun (it never changes form). مَسَاء (masāʾ, evening) — iḍāfa: 'the evening of yesterday.' أَشعُر بِـ (ashʿur bi-, I feel) — Form I of ش-ع-ر (sh-ʿ-r, to feel/sense): always followed by بِـ. تَعَب (taʿab, tiredness) — from root ت-ع-ب (t-ʿ-b, to be tired): تَعِبَ (to tire), تَعبَان (tired, colloquial).

sentences.base_language: Let me take a look at your throat.
sentences.grammarNote: دَعنِي أَفحَص (daʿnī afhiṣ, let me examine) — دَع (Form I imperative of و-د-ع, to let/leave) + suffix نِي (me). The following verb أَفحَص is in the jussive, expressing permission granted. أَفحَص (afhiṣ, I examine) — Form I of ف-ح-ص (f-ḥ-ṣ, to examine/test): فَحَصَ = to examine, فَحص (examination). حَلقَك (ḥalqak, your throat) — حَلق + suffix كَ (your m.).

sentences.base_language: Your throat is a little red. You have a mild infection.
sentences.target_language: حلقك أحمر قليلاً. لديك التهاب خفيف.
sentences.romanized: halqak ahmar qalilan. ladayka iltihab khafif.
sentences.literal: Your-throat red a-little. For-you inflammation mild.
sentences.grammarNote: لَدَيكَ (ladayka) = you have (to a man). This is a more formal alternative to عِندك. The root لَدى behaves slightly differently from عِند: it always takes pronoun suffixes (لَدَيَّ, لَدَيكَ, لَدَيهِ) rather than standing alone.

sentences.base_language: I will prescribe you some medicine.
sentences.grammarNote: سَأَصِف (sa-aṣif, I will prescribe) — Form I of و-ص-ف (w-ṣ-f, to describe/prescribe): وَصَفَ الدَّوَاء = to prescribe medicine (literally 'to describe the medicine'). The initial و drops in the present tense. لَكَ (laka, for you m.) — the preposition لِـ + pronoun suffix. بَعض الدَّوَاء (baʿḍ ad-dawāʾ, some medicine) — بَعض (some/part of) + definite noun in genitive: a partitive construction.

sentences.base_language: Take one tablet three times a day after meals.
sentences.target_language: تناول قرصاً واحداً ثلاث مرات يومياً بعد الوجبات.
sentences.romanized: tanawwal qursan wahidan thalath marrat yawmiyyan ba'd al-wajabat.
sentences.literal: Take tablet one three times daily after the-meals.
sentences.grammarNote: تَنَاوَل (tanāwal, take! — imperative Form VI of ن-و-ل): Form VI تَنَاوَلَ = to take/receive/consume. قُرصاً وَاحِداً (qurṣan wāḥidan, one tablet) — both words in indefinite accusative (tanwin ـاً), noun + adjective agreement. ثَلَاث مَرَّات (thalāth marrāt, three times) — مَرَّات is the sound feminine plural of مَرَّة (time/instance, from root م-ر-ر). يَومِيَّاً (yawmiyyan, daily) — a nisba adjective يَومِيّ + accusative adverb suffix ـاً.

sentences.target_language: هل يجب أن أستريح في البيت؟
sentences.romanized: hal yajib an astaryah fi al-bayt?
sentences.literal: Q must that I-rest in the-house?
sentences.grammarNote: يَجِبُ أَن + present verb = must / should (it is necessary that). This is an impersonal construction: يَجِبُ أَن تَأخُذ = you must take, يَجِبُ أَن نَذهَب = we must go. An informal alternative is لازم (lazim), used widely in spoken Arabic.

sentences.base_language: Yes, rest and drink plenty of water. You will feel better in two or three days.
sentences.target_language: نعم، استرح واشرب الماء الكافي. ستشعر بتحسن خلال يومين أو ثلاثة.
sentences.romanized: na'am, istaryah wa-ishrab al-ma' al-kafi. sa-tash'ur bi-tahassun khilal yawmayn aw thalatha.
sentences.literal: Yes, rest and-drink the-water the-sufficient. Will-you-feel with-improvement within two-days or three.
sentences.grammarNote: اِستَرِح (istariḥ, rest! — Form X imperative of ر-و-ح, to rest): اِستَرَاحَ = to rest/relax. يَومَين (yawmayn, two days) — dual of يَوم (day): the dual suffix ـَين marks exactly two. أَو ثَلَاثَة (aw thalātha, or three) — the alternative. بِتَحَسُّن (bi-taḥassun, with improvement) — Form V verbal noun of ح-س-ن (ḥ-s-n, to be good): تَحَسَّنَ = to improve, تَحَسُّن = improvement.
